The Angular team announced a yearly major cadence this week, and the biggest change is not the version number.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

On July 16, the Angular team opened PR #69817, which does two things: it adds v23 to the release schedule and formally switches Angular from a six-month major cadence to a yearly one. The stated reasons are community demand for less upgrade churn and better API stability for agentic workflows. Both are reasonable. But a slower major cadence reorganizes the planning assumptions every enterprise Angular team has been running on for years, and the announcement does not spell out those second-order effects.
Here is the playbook I am building for my own teams right now.
What This Actually Changes
The six-month clock shaped everything: when you drafted your upgrade PRs, when you negotiated library compatibility with your platform team, and when you could safely pull in new APIs versus waiting one more cycle.
The symptoms teams will hit if they do not update those assumptions:

• Library compatibility windows double. Third-party libraries that soft-dropped a major after one cycle now have more runway, but they also have less pressure to ship Angular 23 support quickly. You cannot assume…
